items.question: How does variance differ from bias in the bias–variance decomposition?
items.answer: Bias² measures systematic error: E_D[ŷ(x)] missing E[y|x]. Variance measures instability: ŷ(x) scattering around E_D[ŷ(x)] when training data changes. High bias is wrong on average; high variance is inconsistent across retrains.

items.question: How does Var(ŷ) in ML differ from Var(X) in probability?
items.answer: Var(X) spreads outcomes of a random variable X around E[X]. Var(ŷ) spreads predictions from models trained on different datasets D around E_D[ŷ]. The randomness is over training samples, not a single draw of X.

items.question: What happens to Var(ŷ) when you increase model flexibility on a fixed small dataset?
items.answer: Variance typically rises: the model fits idiosyncratic noise in that sample, so retraining on a different sample yields very different predictions. Training error may fall while validation error rises—the classic overfitting pattern.

items.question: What is the effect of collecting substantially more training data on variance?
items.answer: More data usually reduces Var(ŷ) because the model sees less sampling noise and estimates stabilize. Learning curves often show validation error falling toward training error as n grows, with bias relatively unchanged.

items.question: What if your decision tree has 100% training accuracy but 78% cross-validation accuracy across 5 folds?
items.answer: That large gap signals high variance (overfitting): the tree memorizes training quirks. Try limiting depth, min_samples_leaf, or switching to a random forest; collect more data if learning curves show validation still improving with n.
